notes: the judgment of the great whore. The image of prostitution was commonly used in the Old Testament to depict extreme apostasy not only from religion but from all that is godly and good (Isa. 1:21; 23:16; Jer. 2:20-31; 13:27; Ezek. 16:15, 28-29; Hosea 2:5; Nahum 3:4). Nephi gives us a definition of who the great whore is: "He that fighteth against Zion, both Jew and Gentile, both bond and free, both male and female, shall perish; for they are they who are the whore of all the earth; for they who are not for me are against me, saith our God" (2 Ne. 10:16). The "judgment of the great whore" means, simply, the punishment of the world of wickedness. The judgment is described in detail in Rev. 17 and 18.(Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 219)

the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication. Drunkenness here is a symbol of apostasy, in which the people lose the power of good judgment because of their unwise choices. "They are drunken, but not with wine," Isaiah prophesied, "they stagger, but not with strong drink" (Isa. 29:9; see Jer. 51:6-9). The wine that makes them drunk is not the fornication of sexuality but that of unfaithfulness to God. (Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 220)

into the wilderness. In Rev. 12 we saw the woman who represented the church of God, the bride of Christ, go into the wilderness for safety. Now we see her counterfeit, the woman who represents the church of the devil, dwelling in the wilderness. In the first instance, the wilderness symbolized a place of refuge; here it symbolizes the dryness and desolation of sin. (Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 221)

The scarlet color of the beast is the same color as the ribbon that was tied around the neck of the scapegoat on the Day of Atonement; the ribbon represented the sins of Israel. In the same way, the scarlet of the beast seems to represent sin (Isa. 1:18). (Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 221)

And the woman was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour. Purple clothing symbolizes royalty (Judg. 8:26), and scarlet clothing symbolizes wealth and nobility (2 Sam. 1:24; Prov. 31:21; Lam. 4:5; Dan. 5:7; Matt. 27:28-29). The woman's clothing indicates her position of wealth and power in the world. (Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 221)

already been done before the Roman Catholic Church came into existence. The Gnostics, the Judaizers, and even Judaism itself had so corrupted the scriptures beforehand that it was the Roman Catholic Church that said, 'My Heavens to Betsy, let's get things tied down before everything's changed.' We should be grateful to the Roman Catholic Church to have received the Bible in as sound a condition as it is today. Oh, of course, it's correct that down through time Monks with their bald heads had to copy all the different scriptures, and marginal references were put here and there and they often made errors in copying, but the intentional corrupting of the materials of gospel scriptures was not done by the Roman Catholic Church. It was done a long time before the Roman Catholic Church came into existence. "Well, you say, 'I know, but it was the Roman Catholic Church that persecuted the Saints of God, wasn't it? So the Roman Catholic Church must then be the Great and Abominable Church.' May I submit to you that it was when the Roman Catholic Church came into existence that the persecution of the Saints ceased. Prior to that time, through Jewish efforts, through Rome, through Gnostics, through apostates, but more especially through the Roman Empire, the persecution of the Christians came about, which caused the blood of martyrs to be sucked up by the goddesses and gods of Rome. It was when the Roman Catholic Church came into existence that toleration began to come to the Saints of God, the accommodation process had taken place by now so that in time there was no difference between church and empire, and peace came to all Christians. "'Ah,' but you say, 'John told us for sure that he was talking about the Pope. Don't you know that the number 666 is referring to the Pope? Therefore, now we have proof that he was talking about the Roman Catholic Church.' I would guess at this point that probably forty-five per cent or more of the Missionary IP Books of all the missionaries in front of me have somewhere in the IP Book either under 'A' for apostasy or 'P' for Pope or 'C' for Catholic or something like this, a page; and on the page would be listed three words, but in vertical shape-- VICARIVS FILII DEI, the 'vicar of God.' By counting numerical equivalent to each of those letters, and using the Latin form of all the letters, you'll see that the V equals 5, and I equals one, the C equals 100, and an A happens to equal 0, (so we'll have to let that go), and R--we'll have to let that go---etc. Then continuing with a D which equals 500, and L equals 50, and sure enough, when we total it all up, excluding those letters that don't count anything--lo and behold, it equals 666. "I would like to submit that there are better interpretations for '666' than the Pope; in fact, I'd like to submit that anything bad or evil has been interpreted to be the number '666.' Someone didn't like Stalin, and so I have it run out here that Stalin equals 666, counting his name in Russian. The first cure of syphilis by Dr. Enlick was coded 666. When the Biblical scholars of the day saw what ridiculous arguments the Seventh-day Adventists were using, taking their theology to make history, which is a dangerous thing, they thought they would have some fun so they took the official name of the Seventh-day Adventist Church in the German language, coded it and out it came, 666. But some said, 'Well, you don't have a man's name; you don't have a person's name,' so they took the name of Helen Harmon White, who was the founder of the Seventh-day Adventists Church and coded her name. It comes out to be 666. I have Hitler, 666; Stalin, 666; the Pope, 666. You see, here's the point: John is talking about evil, he's talking about evilness, and he's pinpointing the evil in his own present time, which is, and I said it was clear as could be, the Emperor of Rome. He was doing all the persecutions. If we had the time I'd like to talk about 666 being the numerical equivalent of the name of the Emperor of Rome. He certainly is not talking about the Roman Catholic Church. "Another so called apostasy scriptures is in II Thessalonians, chapter 2:1-7. You know the day of the Lord will not come until there comes a falling away first and that man of sin be revealed, and the son of perdition who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called of God. Well, Paul is either talking about evil per se' or the Judaizers, or the Roman Empire, or perhaps even the Roman Emperor; but never is he talking about the Roman Catholic Church. "II Timothy, chapter 3:1-7.... This passage is counteracting Gnostics, especially the Libertine Gnostic groups who were active in the first two hundred years of the Christian era. The Libertines were certainly not part of the Roman Catholic Church. "How about I John 4:1-3, where he tells us to believe not every spirit, for there are many spirits

which have come from us and from without us, and they are the spirit of the anti-Christ because they teach that Jesus came not in the flesh. Anyone who knows any of the background of the Gnostic theologians in the early Christian Church knows what John is talking about. In both the Gospel of John and in the Epistles of John he is counteracting Gnosticism, not the Roman Catholic Church. "When rightly understood, all of these so-called Apostasy scriptures are dealing with apostasy, but not the apostasy directly involving the Roman Catholic Church. I am convinced that those who keep perpetuating this doctrine that the Roman Catholic Church is the Great and Abominable Church, have become so influenced by tradition, especially Protestant tradition which has been handed down for the many, many years, that they've failed to scrutinize and analyze the scriptures in their proper setting and in their proper light. I believe one fallacy has been to accuse the Roman Catholic Church as being the producers of the apostasy, and this is not so. That position is unfair to the Roman Catholic Church; she is not the producer of the apostasy. The Roman Catholic Church is what is left over after the apostasy. What produced the apostasy was very complex. It was a blending of many devilish churches and ideologies: the oriental and mystery religions, Judaism, the Judiazers, Pagan Rome, the Roman Imperial Priesthood, the cult of the hero worshipers, the cult of Emperor worship of the Roman Empire, and Gnosticism with all of its various brands and flavors. All of these together produced the apostasy. Therefore, the Roman Catholic Church is , in reality, the picking up of the pieces.... "In reality, the Great and Abominable Church is evilness. It is all that belongs to the Kingdom of Evil. . . . whether it be in Gnosticism, whether it be in the Roman Catholic Church, whether it be in the Mormon Church. I submit that there are people in the Latter-day Saint Church who are members of the Great and Abominable Church, as there are in other churches. "... What has been the greatest evil ever spoken against in very severe words from the pulpit by the President of the Church? Not the Roman Catholic Church, but other ideologies at work on this earth with the great masses of the people. He has characterized Communism as the greatest evil force existing on the earth today, organized as such. "So here, before Nephi's mind, the Lord had divided the world up into two great, broad categories of people; the one group composed the Church of the Lamb of God and the other was the Church of the devil, which must certainly be composed of evil, whether found in the Roman Catholic Church or Pagan Rome or in Babylonian religions or with the Pharaohs in the time of Egypt or with Gadianton and his brethren... "If we turn to section 18 of the Doctrine and Covenants, given before the Church was established, in verse 20 we read that the Lord told the missionaries that they were not to contend against any church, save it be the "church of the devil." The early Mormon missionaries did not go out and attack the Roman Catholic Church. In fact, the Prophet Joseph Smith wrote in his own documentary history, or his own historical journal, that the poor Catholics were not strong enough in America to even defend themselves, and, therefore we ought to uphold them. Never did Joseph Smith suggest that the Roman Catholic Church was the Great and Abominable Church; but, to the contrary, suggested to the Latter-day Saints that we uphold them. He asked that we strive to assure their religious freedom as we would the Methodists or the Presbyterians or our own. "In section 29 of the Doctrine and Covenants, verse 21, the Lord suggests that Ezekiel the Prophet talked about the Great and Abominable Church, even the destruction of the Great and Abominable Church, who was the whore of all the earth. When we read Ezekiel, it is clear that he certainly wasn't writing about the Roman Catholic Church. Ezekiel was writing about the great Northern princes of the heathen nations, Gog and Magog, because to Ezekiel these were the personification of all the evil that existed in Ezekiel's day. And he saw the downfall of evil; just like John saw the downfall of evil, just like Nephi saw the downfall of evil; not just the downfall of any particular church named the Roman Catholic Church or of any other church to be spelled out by name. "John Taylor, an apostle, wrote this article in the Times and Seasons in 1845: "'.....It will readily be perceived in the foregoing extract that John had no more reference to the Roman Catholic and Protestant churches who had a form of godliness, denying the power, than he had to all Babylon from Nimrod down. The old woman, Satan's wife, was drunken with the blood of the

2. She is characterized by the enjoyment of great wealth and luxury (17: 4; 18: 3, 11-16). 3. She (naturally is characterized by sexual immorality (17: 1, 2, 5). 4. She has dominion over all nations (17: 15, 18; 18: 3, 23-24). 5. Her fate is to be consumed by the very kings who have made war on the Lamb under the influence of her deceptions (17: 14-16; 18: 23). "It should be noted that one characteristic not common to both prophetic descriptions is Nephi's statement that the great and abominable church has held back important parts of the canon of scripture. But since John's record is one of the very scriptures Nephi refers to (14: 20-23), this omission in John's account is not surprising. "It must also be noted that in John's Revelation the whore cannot be equated with the two beasts; they do not represent the same things. The whore and the beasts are motivated by the same evil genius, Satan. The one beast supports the whore (Revelation 17: 3), but the beast and the whore are separate entities with separate functions in the evil empire. The whore of Revelation 17-18 is specifically the satanic counterpart of the woman in chapter 12 who symbolizes the Church of Jesus Christ which was forced into the wilderness (that is, she represents both sexual immorality and idolatry, the twin abominations of the Old Testament. It seems that in John's Revelation the symbol of the whore is used narrowly to represent false religion, while the beasts, the image of the beast, and the horns of the beast serve to represent other aspects of the kingdom of the devil. Moreover, if the symbol of the virtuous woman of Revelation 12 is intended to represent specifically the true Church of Jesus Christ (and the crown of Twelve Apostles and her being driven into the wilderness so indicate) it follows that the whore, her counterpart, represents specifically false and counterfeit religion. Satan has more than one institution at his disposal, but the whore is false religion. The whore cannot represent kingdoms or governments--the beast and its horns do that (Revelation 17: 12). But she can represent the false beliefs and ideologies that often capture or motivate governments. The whore provides the theory; government provides the muscle…. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . "It seems to me that many Latter-day Saints have made one of two errors in trying to identify the great and abominable church. The first is to believe that some specific denomination or other, to the exclusion of all others, has been the great and abominable church since the beginning of time. This is dangerous, for if we understand the great and abominable church to be one specific church, some will want to know which one it is, and an antagonistic relationship with that church or denomination will inevitably follow…. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . "…Often it has been suggested that the Roman Catholic Church might be the great and abominable church of 1 Nephi 13, but this is also untenable, primarily because Roman Catholicism as we know it did not yet exist when the crimes described by Nephi were being committed. In fact, the term Roman Catholic makes sense only after A.D. 1054, when it began to be used to distinguish the Western, Latin-speaking Orthodox church, which followed the bishop of Rome, from the Eastern, Greek-speaking Orthodox church, which followed the bishop of Constantinople (in association with others). Indeed, in the period between Peter and Constantine, there were other Christian churches besides the orthodox: Ebionites, Syrian and Egyptian Christians, Donatists, Gnostics, Marcionites, etc. We don't know very much about how they were related to each other. Even if we were to use the term 'Roman' Catholic for the church which Constantine began making his state religion in A.D. 313 (and the other orthodox churches would object to this), still the New Testament as we know it (that is, without the excised plain and precious parts) had already been widely circulated by then. In other words, the work of the great and abominable church in slaying the Apostles and excising the scriptures had already been done. By the time Constantine joined church and state together in the fourth century, the Apostles had been dead for centuries, and the true church and its keys had already been lost. The commonly held notion of shifty-eyed monks rewriting the scriptures as they copied is bigoted and unfair. In fact, we owe those monks a debt of gratitude that anything was saved at all. Besides, in comparison to some of the other Christian groups around, the orthodox Christians had quite a high standard of morality. By this time they

had gone to the extremes of asceticism and can hardly be accused (in this period, anyway) of having many harlots and practicing gross immorality. In fact, in some areas of the ancient world, orthodoxy replaced an earlier more corrupt form of Christianity. Finally, during most of the period before 313, the orthodox were hardly in a position to persecute the Saints, as they were being thrown to the lions themselves. "The Catholic (that is, 'universal') Church of the fourth century was the result of the Apostasy, its end product--not its cause. To find the real culprits in the case of the excised texts, we need to look at a much earlier period in Christian church history. None of the Presidents of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Saints has ever identified Roman Catholicism as the great and abominable church. And, in speaking of Catholic and Protestant faiths, the Prophet Joseph Smith said: "'The old Catholic church traditions are worth more than all you have said. Here is a principle of logic that most men have no more sense than to adopt. I will illustrate it by an old apple tree. Here jumps off a branch and says, I am the true tree, and you are corrupt. If the whole tree is corrupt, are not its branches corrupt? If the Catholic religion is a false religion, how can any true religion come out of it? If the Catholic church is bad, how can any good thing come out of it? The character of the old churches have always been slandered by all apostates since the world began.' (HC 6: 478.) "It was Martin Luther, not Joseph Smith, who identified the Roman Catholic church as Babylon and the Pope as Antichrist. Besides, are we really to believe that Satan had no ministers in the world before there were Roman Catholics? Was there no Babylon to oppose Zion in the days of Cain, Nimrod, Pharaoh, or Herod? "Finally, I would like to submit that no single historical church or denomination known to us can be the great and abominable church in an exclusive sense. No single organization meets all the requirements… . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . "To return to our original topic, can we identify the historical agency that acted as the great and abominable church in earliest Christianity and which Nephi and others describe? I would like to argue that the great and abominable church Nephi describes in chapter 13 had its origins in the second half of the first century and had essentially done its work by the middle of the second century. This period might be called the blind spot of ecclesiastical history, for it is here that the fewest primary historical sources have been preserved. Essentially, what happened is that we have good sources for New Testament Christianity (the New Testament documents themselves); then the lights go out (that is, we have very few historical sources), and in the dark we hear the muffled sounds of a great struggle. When the lights come on again a hundred years or so later, we find that someone has rearranged all the furniture and that Christianity is something very different from what it was in the beginning. That different entity can be accurately described by the term Hellenized Christianity. The Hellenization of Christianity is a phenomenon which has long been recognized by scholars of Christian history, but it is one which Latter-day Saints know better as the Great Apostasy. Hellenization means imposing Greek culture on the native cultures of the East. The result was a synthesis of East and West, with elements of the Greek West predominating, a melting-pot, popular culture which was virtually worldwide. "But in the realm of religion, synthesis means compromise, and when we speak in terms of the gospel, compromise with the popular culture of the world means apostasy from the truth. When Jewish Christianity and Greek culture met head-on in the gentile mission field in the middle of the first century, the Greeks eventually won, and Jewish Christianity was ultimately 'revised' to make it more attractive and appealing to a Greek audience. Primary prejudices of the Hellenistic world were the 'absolute' nature of God (that is, he cannot be bound or limited by anything) and the impossibility of anything material or physical being eternal. In order to accommodate these ideas and thus appeal to a broader gentile audience, Christianity had to discard the doctrines of an anthropomorphic God and the resurrection of the dead or else 'reinterpret' them in a manner that had the same effect…." (Stephen E. Robinson, Early Christianity and 1 Nephi 13-14," in The Book of Mormon: First Nephi, The Doctrinal Foundation, Second Annual Book of Mormon Symposium 1987, pp. 177-191.)

Babylon is the name of the spirit that comes over those who kill the prophets. That spirit has infected much of the world. (See commentary on 16:6; also see 18:24; Matt. 23:34-35; 2 Cor. 4:8-11; Heb. 9:16.) The word drunken suggests that through the ages the righteous have been victims of a great slaughter-a slaughter that was intoxicating to the perpetrators. (Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1998], 224)

Robert Mounce explains how Rome could be the harlot by reminding that, "In the OT prophetic discourse the imagery of the harlot is commonly used to denote religious apostasy. Isaiah laments that the once faithful Jerusalem has become a harlot (Isa. 1: 21). Jeremiah speaks of Israel's 'adulteries and neighings' (elsewhere she is a wild ass in heat, Jer. 2: 24) and 'lewd harlotries' (Jer. 13: 27; cf. Jer. 2: 20-31; Ezek. 16: 15ff; Hos. 2: 5). Since the harlot of the Apocalypse is a pagan city (cf. 17: 18), it is more likely that a passage like Nahum 3: 4 or Isaiah 23: 16, 17 supplies the immediate background. In the former, the harlot is Nineveh, who betrays nations with her harlotries and her charms (cf. Rev. 17: 4). Isaiah pictures Tyre as a forgotten harlot. In the context of Revelation 17 and 18 the imagery is not that of religious profligacy but of the prostitution of all that is right and noble for the questionable ends of power and luxury. Whether Jezebel or Cleopatra sat for the portrait John is now painting makes little difference. The harlot is Rome. Adorned in luxury and intoxicated with the blood of the saints, she stands for a dominant world system based on seduction for personal gain over against the righteous demands of a persecuted minority. John's images are timeless in that they portray the essential conflicts of mankind from the beginning of time until the end. At the close of history the great harlot stands as the final and intensified expression of worldly power." (Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New Intrnl. Commentary, pp. 307-8.)

Elder Mc Conkie states that the woman is "Rome, the capital city of the Babylonish Church." [MLM 440.] Others have suggested that the woman is "Roma, the goddess of Rome. She sits on Rome. The scarlet coloured beast, according to this explanation, is the whole Roman Empire. This is possibly born out by the suggestion that the "seven heads = the seven hills (Rome and seven emperors. One head 'was' (Nero), 'is not' (Nero was certainly dead), 'yet is' (Nero Redivivus)." [Reed C. Durham, "Revelations: The Plainest Book Ever Written," New Era, May 1973.] Stephen Robinson suggests that she is Hellenistic Christianity, which was tied very closely to the Roman Empire. [Stephen E. Robinson, Early Christianity and 1 Nephi 13-14," in The Book of Mormon: First Nephi, The Doctrinal Foundation, Second Annual Book of Mormon Symposium 1987, pp. 177-191.]
(Scarlet colored beast--is this the same beast as in chapter 13 [nations inspired by Satan]--JST Rev. 13: 1.)

This is obviously a parody of the Lamb of God, who was put to death yet came back to life and now is alive forevermore (Rev. 1: 18; 2: 8). It is also in opposition to the one "who is and who was and who is to come" (Rev. 1: 4, 8; 4: 8). In a broader sense, this is the beast of chapter 13 who, although having received the stroke of death, returns to hurl himself with renewed fury against the forces of God. It is this incredible power of resuscitation that causes those who dwell upon the earth to stand in awe." (Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New Intrnl. Commentary, p. 312.) He is the same beast who had received a death-stroke in one of his heads and yet survived (13: 3, 12, 14). Though the beast itself is representative of kingdoms of this world (JST 13: 1), the inspiration of the beast is from the one who long ago promised to purchase kings, priests, etc. Throughout history, he repeatedly shows up in imitation of God. He claims to be the God of this world. When he is turned out, he returns. Even when he is bound, he will be loosed for one final short season.

"The seven heads of the beast are first identified as seven mountains upon which the harlot is sitting. There is little doubt that a first-century reader would understand this reference in any way other than as a reference to Rome, the city built upon seven hills. Rome began as a network of seven hill settlements on the left bank of the Tiber, and was from the time of Servious Tullius (her sixth king)…" (Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New Intrnl. Commentary, pp. 313-14.)

"This seems to be a clear allusion to Rome, the city founded on seven hills, as the headquarters of that branch of Satan's kingdom which was persecuting and slaying the saints in John's day." (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, DNTC 3: 554.)

be included in any list. "One at present exists. This must be Vespasian, the first emperor to bring back stability to the empire, after the chaos following the death of Nero; he reigned from 79-81. "The beast which was, and is not, it itself the eighth. It proceeds from the series of the seven, and is on its way to destruction. This can only mean that the emperor who followed Titus is being identified with Nero redivivus and Antichrist; and the emperor who followed Titus was Domitian. "Can Domitian reasonably be identified with the evil force which Nero redivivus personified? We turn to the life of Domitian written by Suetonius the Roman biographer, remembering that Suetonius was not a Christian. Domitian, as Suetonius tells, was an object of terror and hatred to all. We get a grim picture of him at the beginning of his reign. 'He used to spend hours in seclusion every day, doing nothing but catch flies and stab them with a keenly-sharpened stylus.' Any psychologist would find that a curiously revealing picture. He was insanely jealous and insanely suspicious. He formed a homosexual attachment for a famous actor called Paris. One of the pupils of Paris so much resembled his teacher that it was not unreasonable to suppose that he was his son; the lad was promptly murdered. Hermogenes, the historian, wrote things which Domitian did not like; he was executed, and the scribe who had copied the manuscript was crucified. Senators were slaughtered right and left. Sallustius Lucullus, governor of Britain, was executed because he allowed a new type of lance to be called Lucullan…. "To all this we may add a final fact; it was Domitian who first made Caesar worship compulsory and who was, therefore, responsible for unloosing the flood-tides of persecution of the Christian Church. "It might well be that John saw in Domitian the reincarnation (John did not believe in reincarnation, but may have thought of Domitian as being possessed of the same Satan who possessed Nero) of Nero. Others did precisely the same. Juvenal spoke of Rome being 'enslaved to a bald-headed Nero' (Domitan was bald) was exiled and finally murdered for his temerity. Tertullian called Domitian 'a man of Nero's type of cruelty,' and 'a sub-Nero,' a verdict which Eusebius repeated. "The one difficulty is that it makes it look as if John wrote in the reign of Vespasian; and we know that John in fact wrote under Domitian. Two possibilities may explain this. John may have written this particular vision years before in the time of Vespasian, lived to see it come terribly true and incorporated it in his final draft of the Revelation. Or he may himself back into the time of Vespasian to trace in retrospect the terrible lines that history had taken." (William Barclay, The Revelation of John, Vol. 2, pp. 139-141.) Robert Mounce examines the difficulties of these verses and finally explains that "Perhaps the most satisfactory explanation of the seven kings is that the number seven is primarily symbolic and stands for the power of the tabulation of the past but is declaring the nearness of the end by the commonly accepted use of a numerical scheme. For John seven is the number of completeness. Five kings have fallen, one is, and the last one remains for only a short time. In other apocalyptic writings the number of ages or world periods conforms to different numerical schemes.… The single purpose of the apocalyptists in all such number schemes is to declare the imminent end of the age. In Revelation the seven kings represent the entire period of Roman domination regardless of the exact number of emperors. The important point is that the end is drawing near. Caird writes, 'The one point John wishes to emphasize is that the imperial line has only a short time to run before the emergence of a new monstrous Nero, an eighth who is one of the seven' (p. 219)." (Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New Intrnl. Commentary, pp. 315-16.)

Vespasian A.D. 69 -- A.D. 79 Titus A.D. 79 -- A.D. 81 Domitian A.D. 81 -- A.D. 96 (Revelation is written) [Reed C. Durham, Revelations: The Plainest Book Ever Written, New Era, May 1973.] As indicated above, Durham sides with most biblical scholars in indicating that verses ten and eleven deal again (like chapter thirteen) with the Nero Redivivus Myth. Verse eleven is easier to fit with that concept than verse ten. Domitian, the emperor of John's time (maybe), is the eighth in line, (not allowing the three pretenders). Verse 10 is more difficult. Barclay presents a traditional scholarly view, with the admission that it is not a clean fit. "Five have fallen. The Roman Empire began with August; and the first five emperors were Augustus, Tiberius, Caligula, Claudius, and Nero. These, then, are the five who have fallen. We have already seen that after the death of Nero there were two years of chaos in which Galba, Otho and Vitellius followed each other in quick succession. They were not in any real sense emperors and cannot

Verse ten is generally interpreted in the historical perspective of the Nero Redivivus concept. This may, in fact, be an accurate reflection of John's current sociopolitical scene. Keep in mind, however, that the real value in understanding the historical background of the scriptures is to shed light on how they impact on our day. John's vision, particularly, is of things in the future (seventh seal). Reed Durham is one or several Mormon scholars who uses the Nero Redivivus idea as an aid to understand some of what John is saying. The following list of emperors is taken form his writings. In verse eleven of chapter seventeen, Durham interprets the: One head that 'was' as Nero, the head that 'is not' means that Nero was certainly dead, and yet ' is' as Nero Redivivus. Augustus 27 B.C. -- A.D. 14 (Christ Born) Tiberius A.D. 14 -- A.D. 37 (Christ's ministry and death) Caligula A.D. 37 -- A.D. 41 Claudius A.D. 41 -- A.D. 54 Nero A.D. 54 -- A.D. 68 (Nero Redivivus myth)

I would add to Caird's point that the same pattern should be expected to be found in the seventh seal, else why go through all the hassle of understanding. Remember, John's record is about the future more than it is about his day.

One speculative interpretation is offered by the Protestant scholar, Robert Mounce. Just as Babylon is a symbol of sin (DNTC 3: 559), this beast which is personified by being the eighth head,"John now arrives at the heart of the riddle. The beast himself is an eighth king who is at the same time one of the seven. If the seven kings were specific Roman emperors, then the most likely candidate for number eight would be Domitian. This would identify the beast as the emperor reigning at the time John receives his vision. Yet three times in the chapter we are told that the beast is not (17: 8 [twice], 11). At the time of writing, the beast has not yet ascended from the abyss (17: 8). He is an eighth in the sense that he is distinct from the other seven. He is Antichrist, not simply another Roman emperor. He is not a human ruler through whom the power of evil finds expression--he is that evil power itself. He belongs to the cosmic struggle between God and Satan which lies behind the scenes of human history. Ye he will appear on the stage of history as a man. He is of the seven--not one of the seven--in that he plays the same sort of role as his earthly predecessors. He himself, however, belongs to another sphere of reality. His period of hegemony is the greater tribulation preceding the return of the Messiah. This interpretation requires no reliance upon the Nero Redivivus myth, although its existence would aid in the understanding of John's prophecy of Antichrist. Nero, the epitome of evil and the abuse of power, was (he lived and ruled) and is not (that is, he is now dead). Yet (according to the myth) he will return to life and once again seize power. Commentators who understand the eighth king as Domitian stress that early writers recognized a resemblance between Nero and Domitian. But since all the beast's heads were part of the same imperial system through which the evil intent of Antichrist found expression, we would of course expect the two prominent persecutors to be comparable." (Robert Mounce, "The Book of Revelation," The New Intrnl. Commentary, p. 316.)

Remember that the Prophet Joseph indicated that the beasts were in the likeness of kingdoms. (JST 13: 1.) Mounce, if he is right, seems to suggest that verse eleven pinpoints a symbolic personification of the beast. For John's time, this could very well have been a Nero Redivivus. An example of evil personified in our time would be Hitler. Often, tyrants are measured by his standards of evil.

One author identifies these ten kings as "purely eschatological figures representing the totality of the powers of all nations on the earth which are to be made subservient" to Satan. Recall that the number ten is symbolic and indicates completeness. It does not point to ten specific kings nor to ten European kingdoms of a revived Roman empire. (I. T. Beckwith, The Apocalypse of John, p. 700.)
This is an excellent insight in that it reminds us that though John's vision uses typology and metaphors from his day, it is a vision of "that which is to come."

We find that after the days of Noah an order was introduced called the patriarchal order, in which every man managed his own family affairs. And prominent men among them were kings and priests unto God, and officiated in what is known among us as the priesthood of the Son of God, or the priesthood after the order of Melchizedek. Man began again to multiply on the face of the earth, and the heads of families became their kings and priests, that is, the fathers of their own people. And they were more or less under the influence and guidance of the Almighty. We read, for instance, in our revelations pertaining to these matters, of a man called Melchizedek, who was a great high priest. We are told that there were a great many high priests in his day, and before him and after him; and these men had communication with God, and were taught of him in relation to their general proceedings, and acknowledged the hand of God in all things which they were associated. (John Taylor, The Gospel Kingdom, 139.)

It would be an error to blame some modern denomination for the activities of an ancient great and abominable church. The other error is to go too far the other way, dehistoricizing the abominable church altogether. The term then becomes merely a vague symbol for all the disassociated evil in the world. We cannot, in the face of the scriptural evidence, accept this view. For if we do, we shall not be able to recognize the categories and know who is playing the role of Babylon in our own times or in times to come. Thus, we must, on the one hand, avoid the temptation to identify the role of the great and abominable church so completely with one particular entity that we do not recognize the part when it is played by some other entity. At the same time, we must remember that the role will be played by some entity or coalition, and we must be able to tell by their characteristic fruits which is Zion and which is Babylon.

By the time of Constantine, the Apostles had been dead for centuries. Furthermore, the early Orthodox church can hardly be accused of immorality. It had, in fact, gone to the extremes of asceticism. In some areas of the world Orthodoxy replaced an earlier, already corrupt form of Christianity. And during much of the period, members of the Orthodox Church were not in a position to persecute anyone, as they were being thrown to the lions themselves. The Catholic church of the fourth century was the result of the Apostasy—its end product—not the cause. To find the real culprits, we need to look at a much earlier period in church history than the fourth century after Christ. Satan had his ministers in the world long before then, and we must remember that Babylon was already there to oppose Zion in the days of Cain, Nimrod, Pharaoh, and Herod.

Another symbol used in the book of Revelation to represent the great and abominable church, as well as worldliness and wickedness in general, is Babylon. Five of the six characteristics identified in 1 Nephi are also attributed to Babylon in the book of Revelation: 1. Babylon is drunk with the blood of the Saints, the martyrs of Jesus, and the prophets. (See Rev. 17:6; Rev. 18:24.) 2. She is known for her enjoyment of great wealth and luxury. (See Rev. 17:4; Rev. 18:3, 11–16.) 3. She is characterized by wanton sexual immorality. (See Rev. 17:1–2, 5.) 4. She has dominion over all nations. (See Rev. 17:15, 18; Rev. 18:3, 23–24.) 5. Her fate is to be consumed by the very kings who, because of her deceptions, have made war on the Lamb. (See Rev. 17:14–16; Rev. 18:23.)

quotes.text: cult chores to be done, ironies and adversities to be experienced, time to be well spent, talents and gifts to be well employed. Just because we were chosen “there and then,” surely does not mean we can be indifferent “here and now.” Whether foreordination for men, or foredesignation for women, those called and prepared must also prove “chosen, and faithful” (See Revelation 17:14; D&C 121:34–36.)
quotes.attr: Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Oct. 1985, 21; or Ensign, Nov. 1985, 17

quotes.text: be made the king and lawgiver to my family, and if I have many sons, I shall become the father of many fathers, for they will have sons, and their sons will have sons, and so on, from generation to generation, and, in this way, I may become the father of many fathers, or the king of many kings. This will constitute every man a prince, king, lord, or whatever the Father sees fit to confer upon us.

he mouth of God, magnifying their callings, going from grace to grace, until through the fulness of the ordinances of the temple they receive the fulness of the priesthood and are ordained kings and priests. Those so attaining shall have exaltation and be kings, priests, rulers, and lords in their respective spheres in the eternal kingdoms of the great King who is God our Father. (Rev 1:6; 5:10.)
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, "Kings," Mormon Doctrine, 425.

quotes.text: The Gnostics wallowed in the mysteries and in unspeakable practices on the one hand or in neurotic asceticism on the other. Second-century compilers like Tatian and Marcion rewrote the scriptures, the latter boldly chopping out anything he didn’t like. And all of them together forced the virtuous woman, the true church of Jesus Christ, into the wilderness.
quotes.attr: Stephen E. Robinson, “Warring against the Saints of God,” Ensign, Jan 1988, 34

notes: "The church of the devil is every evil and worldly organization on earth. It is all of the systems, both Christian and non-Christian, that have perverted the pure and perfect gospel; it is all of the governments and powers that run counter to the divine will; it is the societies and political parties and labor unions that sow strife and reap contention. It is communism; it is Islam; it is Buddhism; it is modern Christianity in all its parts. It is Germany under Hitler, Russia under Stalin, and Italy under Mussolini. It is the man of sin speaking in churches, orating in legislative halls, and commanding the armies of men. And its headquarters are everywhere--in Rome and Moscow, in Paris and London, in Teheran and Washington--everywhere that evil forces, either of church or state or society, can be influenced. The immanent and all-pervading presence of evil in high places is one of the signs of the times." (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, The Millennial Messiah pp. 54-55)

sea beast. The beast in chapter thirteen had ten horns with ten crowns. These had evidently received their kingdoms already (in John's vision). The ten horns of chapter seventeen were kings without their own kingdom. This could have had some significance to John's day in that "The Nero legends anticipated the return of Nero from the east with a great army to destroy his enemies in Rome (Sib. Or. 4: 138-39; 5: 93-110, 361-85). During the period of the late republic (133-41 B.C.), Rome experience almost continual internal disorder brought on by a relatively independent army of professional soldiers under the control of strong leaders, some of whom captured the city. When Sulla captured Rome in 82 B.C., he had thousands of his opponents murdered. With the recent instability of the year of the three emperors (A.D.68-69), the vengeance on Rome associated with Nero's return had ample historical precedent." (Harper's Bible Commentary, p. 1315.)

The most important story told in these verses is how, in the final analysis, Satan will not support his own. (Alma 30: 60.) We can see quite clearly that it is by the wicked that the wicked are punished. (Mormon 4:5.)
Cross reference with verse 12. Notice that these ten horns are unlike the ten horns of chapter thirteen's
It is quite clear in this verse that the Lord can use all, righteous or wicked, in bringing about his eternal purposes. (See also Mormon 4:5 and Moses 4:6.)

"Again this seems to be a clear allusion to Rome, the capital city and center of civil autocracy, social degeneracy, and spiritual disease and death. 'The state of society at Rome, at the time of the Revelation,' Dummelow says, 'was probably the worst the world had ever seen.' This conclusion is, of course, an exaggeration since the most degenerate social circumstances were those of Noah's day. But Dummelow's description is apt in pointing up the difficulties facing the meridian saints. He continues: 'The aristocracy, which alone had any voice in public affairs, was, with few exceptions, utterly given over to the most shameless wickedness. Vast wealth was in their hands, which was spent in unbridled luxury and debauchery. Their continual craving for new sensations was ministered to by foreign parasites, who introduced new vices and flagrant superstitions. With no feeling for others, their cruelty was appalling. With their appetite for life jaded by the pursuit of pleasure, suicide became common. The herded masses of the people were sunk in ignorance and pauperism. The public distribution of corn confirmed them in idleness, and the public shows helped to harden their hearts and to corrupt their feelings. The state religion was not believed in by the educated, while it had no moral teaching to provide for those who did believe in it, and there was no system of public education.' (Dummelow, p. 1086.)" [Bruce R. Mc Conkie, DNTC 3: 555-6.]

The Babylonian captivity was perhaps the lowest point of Israel's history. Elder Bruce R. Mc Conkie wrote: "Both Isaiah and Jeremiah spoke at length of the devastations of that day and of the cursings and destruction that would come upon Babylon as a result. (Isa. 13:1 and 21; Jer. 50:1 and 51.) Babylon was truly the great enemy of the Lord's people anciently, and her overthrow and the destruction of her worldliness and wickedness was one of the things of greatest interest and concern to them. What was more natural, then, than for John and all the prophets to use Babylon as the symbol of sin, and her destruction as the overthrow of wickedness on earth." (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Doctrinal New Testament Commentary, 3:559.)
